European Road Impressions  ·  1979 Model Year

The Ghostin Ice Green

1979 Porsche 911 SC Targa  ·  European Specification
Paint Code 266  ·  Factory Bilstein Sports Suspension
There are cars that perform. There are cars that communicate. And then, very rarely, there is a car that conspires with you.
Carveyance  ·  Road Impressions  ·  May 2026  ·  European 930/03

It begins before you even open the door. The color stops people in their tracks — Ice Green Metallic, paint code 266, is not so much a shade as a phenomenon. In overcast northern European light, it reads almost silver, cool and restrained, the color of old pewter or frozen Baltic water. Step into full afternoon sun and something shifts: faint mint undertones rise from the flake, shimmering with an almost bioluminescent quality that no paint catalogue photograph has ever captured honestly. This is not Guards Red announcing itself from a hundred meters. This is a car that reveals itself slowly, on its own terms. And for those who understand what they are looking at, that restraint is worth more than any color that shouts.

The SC Targa is equally particular about its silhouette. The Targa bar, black and substantial, bisects the roofline with architectural precision, creating a shape that the coupe — lovely as it is — simply cannot match for visual drama. The removable roof panel stows easily, and with it gone, what remains is something that feels simultaneously open and purposeful, the car's flanks curving wide at the rear in those signatures flared arches, the Fuchs wheels framed perfectly beneath. This is the G-series body at its most assured. It is a shape that Pininfarina's finest work of the same era — the Ferrari Dino 246, perhaps the most beautiful Italian car of the 1970s — could not make you forget. They were rivals once, equally priced, equally celebrated in their respective ways. The Dino got the Italian romance. The Porsche got the road.

The European Difference

The Car as Intended

To understand what this car is, you must first understand what it isn't. The 911 SC sold in America in 1979 was a compromised machine — its 930/04 engine strangled by emissions equipment, a two-way catalytic converter adding roughly 30 kilograms of dead weight, its power measured in SAE net figures that told a different story than the DIN ratings Europeans read in their brochures. The American car's top speed was quoted at around 126 mph. Its speedometer, mandated by the US Department of Transportation, refused to display anything above 85 mph. Not because the car could not exceed it. Because a bureaucrat decided drivers should not know they could.

The European 911 SC you are reading about here suffered none of this. Its Type 930/03 engine — the engine Porsche designed for this car — breathed freely, uncorrupted by catalysts beyond the basic air injection system all SCs carried. Its speedometer swept to 260 km/h and felt no obligation to stop short of it. Its headlights were proper H4 European units that illuminated the road. At the time, these distinctions were largely invisible to American buyers who never had the chance to compare. Today, they represent an entirely different driving proposition — and a materially rarer car.

Specification Comparison  ·  1979 Porsche 911 SC Targa

The Sports Suspension Option

Telepathic by Factory Specification

This particular car was specified with the factory Bilstein sports suspension — a decision that transforms an already engaging car into something approaching telepathic. The standard SC Targa rode on Boge dampers, perfectly adequate for a German autobahn or the roads of southern France but calibrated for comfort first and communication second. The Bilstein units — stiffer in valving, more directly damped — change the conversation entirely.

The Targa presents a specific engineering challenge that the Bilstein option addresses with particular elegance. Unlike the coupe, whose roof structure provides a continuous hoop of rigidity, the Targa bar and removable panel leave the chassis more susceptible to flex — what Porsche engineers and club racers call 'the Targa twist.' The factory Bilstein specification threads this needle correctly: firmer than stock, meaningfully so, but not so aggressive that the Targa's inherent character is violated. The result is a car that feels set on rails without feeling like a punishment on imperfect pavement.

Behind the Wheel

The Ritual of Starting

It begins the moment you reach for the door handle. That distinctive metallic click of the release mechanism — precise, unhurried, the sound of something engineered rather than assembled — tells you something before you have even looked inside. It tells you it is going to be a good day. That you are about to step into something no longer made of this world. The leather cuff of the door, as you pull it open, carries a scent that is part archive, part workshop, part decades of careful preservation — a smell that belongs exclusively to properly kept air-cooled Porsches of this era and cannot be replicated by anything produced after it. You must experience it to understand it. Words make it smaller.

Settle into the driver's seat and the first thing you notice is how close everything is. Not uncomfortably so — this is not a cramped cockpit — but intimately. The leather is everywhere: thick, properly grained, applied with the attention of people who understood that the driver's hands and back and legs would live against it for thousands of miles, and it needed to be worth that time. The craftsmanship is not decorative. It is structural. It holds up because it was made to hold up, and sitting in it now, in the second half of the 1970s Porsche's life, the interior still feels correct in a way that modern cabins — with their acres of carefully textured plastic and their ambient lighting packages — simply do not.

The dials are placed exactly where they should be. The tachometer dominates, large and unambiguous, positioned directly behind the leather-wrapped steering wheel exactly as it should be in any car that takes seriously the idea that the driver should be reading the engine rather than managing a menu. VDO instruments, precise and Germanic, surrounding it — each one telling you what it knows and nothing else. Your eyes wander to the right and there they are: the climate control knobs, sliders rather than dials, satisfyingly tactile in the way that physical controls always are, and touchscreens never will be. Below them, an old cassette player that has absolutely no business working as well as it does and yet does — playing whatever tape you fed it with a fidelity that, in this car, on this road, feels entirely sufficient.

The three-spoke steering wheel, if this car was specified with the optional 380mm leather-rimmed item, fills your hands with something that feels sculpted rather than manufactured. The key lives to the left of the column, a quirk inherited from Porsche's Le Mans origins, where left-hand starts meant a driver could be running across the pit lane to a car already turned to its left-hand ignition. It remains the most civilized start ritual in motoring.

The seats themselves deserve a specific word. Comfortable in the way that properly designed things from this era are comfortable — not padded into submission, not sculpted with algorithmic precision into some idealized human shape but contoured in a way that proves someone sat in them and thought carefully about who would spend time here. They could stand more bolster support for hard driving — that is the honest admission — but what they offer instead is a naturalness of fit, a sense that your body belongs in this particular bucket, that compensates considerably. The seat travel is remarkable — slide it fully rearward and it feels like descending into something, a shaft of space that accommodates even the tallest drivers in a way that announces the German engineering preference for practicality over fashion.

With the roof removed — it stows neatly in the folded rear seats or in the front luggage compartment, either working without drama, either feeling like exactly the right solution — the car becomes what it was always meant to be. Open to the sky, narrowed to its essential purpose, stripped of everything that was ever optional about the experience.

Turn the key. The 3.0-litre air-cooled flat-six catches with a mechanical clatter that resolves, within seconds, into something between a purr and a bark. It is an engine sound without contemporary equivalent — layers of mechanical conversation audible behind you, each cylinder contributing its voice to a sound that engages before you have moved a meter. The engine does not warm silently. It tells you it is warming. This is not noise. This is communication.

There is a moment, just after the flat-six catches and settles into its warm-up idle, when the engine behind you and the sky above you and the Ice Green on either side of you create a specific quality of feeling that it would take a thesaurus to properly describe. Driving was an art form once. Special cars were made. This is both of those things, in its original form, with nothing added and nothing taken away. The key turning left of the column — thank you, Le Mans — is not merely a quirk or a historical footnote. It is the ceremony that begins the whole thing. You participate in it rather than simply starting a vehicle.

Engine Character  ·  930/03 3.0-Litre Flat-Six

A Map of the Conversation

Idle – 2,000Breathing
The engine settles and warms. A mechanical heartbeat, unhurried. Tractable, pulling gently from walking pace. In town, in traffic, you barely need to ask it anything.
2,000 – 3,500Torque Territory
Peak torque arrives at 4,200 rpm but its buildup is already muscular through this range. The car rolls onto the throttle with authority. This is the everyday sweet spot — the range where the 915 gearbox finds its rhythm and long-distance cruising becomes meditative.
3,500 – 5,000The Conversation Begins
The exhaust note hardens and deepens. The car becomes urgent, pressing you back into the seat with growing insistence. The rear-engine weight begins to work in your favor on corner exits, loading the rear tires as the engine pulls from behind. This is where the car begins to truly talk.
5,000 – 5,500Peak Power
This is where 180 DIN horsepower arrives, honest and uncorrupted. The flat-six is singing at full voice. The Targa bar frames the sky above you, the road rushing beneath. Time compresses. This is what the car was engineered for — not the number on the speedometer, but this precise quality of engagement.
Above 5,500Into the Redline
The revs are there to be used, but the SC rewards precision more than brute force. Experienced drivers find the zone between 4,500 and 5,500 to be where the car is most alive, most communicative, most itself.

The Rear-Engine Dialogue

Much mythology surrounds the handling of the early 911, and like most mythology it contains a kernel of truth wrapped in considerable exaggeration. The rear-engine layout does create a car with different weight distribution to any front-engine machine, and that difference must be respected. But the SC was already a generation removed from the truly challenging early 911s. Porsche had worked, across successive models, to civilize the dynamic without neutering the character, and by 1979 they had largely succeeded.

The golden rule is one that experienced 911 drivers know by instinct: maintain throttle through corners. The rear-engine mass that can become a liability under abrupt lift-off becomes, with correct technique, an ally — loading the rear tires, generating traction, allowing the car to rotate with a grace that front-engine cars, however sophisticated, cannot quite replicate. Lift off mid-corner and the weight transfers forward sharply; the rear unloads and the tail begins to seek the outside of the curve. This is not malice. It is physics. And it is what separates the drivers who merely operate a 911 from those who drive one.

The unassisted steering requires honest work at low speeds — parking, tight turns, the slow-speed maneuvering that power assistance makes effortless in modern cars. But this is not a hardship. It is a feature, though not everyone will immediately recognize it as one. Because at speed, at the speed this car was designed to be used, the unassisted steering becomes something else entirely: an ally. A direct, unfiltered connection to the contact patches that no power-assisted system has ever convincingly replicated. You feel the road through your palms. You feel the tires loading and unloading in real time. The car feels like a go-kart. It feels like it is on rails. The low weight — just over 1,200 kilograms, you feel the lack of it in every direction change — and the rear-engine mass working with you rather than against you creates a dynamic that is specific to this car and this configuration and nothing else.

The speed, when it comes, surprises you. It does not feel particularly rapid below the meat of the torque and power curve — the 930/03 is a smooth, tractable engine that pulls cleanly from low down without drama. But enter the powerband properly, somewhere between 3,500 and 5,500 rpm, and the SC becomes rapid in the way that naturally aspirated high-revving cars uniquely manage: immediate, building, vocal. The air-cooled engine climbs through the revs with a mechanical honesty that turbocharged modern engines cannot replicate. You are using the gearbox constantly — the 915's throws are long and deliberate, requiring intention rather than flicks, meaning your left leg is genuinely working and genuinely contributing. It is worth every shift. With the Bilstein sports suspension finding every apex and the flat-six singing behind you and the Ice Green flanks blurring in your peripheral vision, you feel in total control, completely alive, and wearing a grin that the situation has placed on your face without your full consent.

The brakes — 282mm ventilated discs front, 290mm rear — are honest without being extraordinary. They require planning ahead and firm, progressive input. There is no ABS to catch your mistakes. This is a car that asks you to be precise.

“There's a certain raw, mechanical sound and feel to these engines that modern water-cooled engines just can't replicate. It's a symphony of mechanical noises that engage your senses — the torque curve nicely spread, making it feel responsive and eager, whether you were cruising or pushing it hard.”

— Contemporary Driver Account, Air-Cooled 911 SC

Newer 911s are faster. More comfortable. Better damped. More refined in every measurable category. These are facts and they are true and they are also, for the purposes of this conversation, entirely beside the point. Because what the modern cars have gained in capability they have surrendered in simplicity — in the particular quality of fear that makes you feel genuinely skilled when you get something right rather than merely well-supported when the electronics prevent you from getting it wrong. The power, the weight, the speed profile of the SC is perhaps comparable to a current Mazda MX-5 in circuit trim. But even the MX-5 in its rawest club form cannot quite harness what this car offers — cannot quite match the 911 SC in all its entirely unfiltered, air-cooled, forty-five-year-old glory. The gap is not in the numbers. It is in the experience of the thing. Some cars make you faster. The 911 SC makes you better.

Context and Rivals

The Dino Conversation — and What the Market Proved

The Ferrari Dino 246 and the Porsche 911 were the two defining European sports cars of their era — natural rivals in price, purpose, and philosophy, separated by everything that Italy and Germany stood for respectively. When the Dino 246 GT arrived in the US, it carried an MSRP of approximately $13,900 — comparable to the 911 of the period. Two cars. Same price bracket. Radically different answers to the same question.

The Dino had the badge argument — or rather, the fascinating absence of one. Enzo Ferrari refused to put the prancing horse on the car. A brochure once noted it was 'almost a Ferrari.' Pininfarina designed the body. Its mid-mounted 2.4-litre V6 screamed to redline with operatic urgency. It was possibly the most beautiful two-seat sports car produced in the 1970s.

And then the market passed its verdict. Today, Ferrari Dino 246 GTs carry an average sales price of $355,755 with the highest recent sale reaching $582,246. The 911 SC averages $63,214 with exceptional examples reaching $515,000 at auction in November 2025. But here is what that market split tells you: the Dino is now a trophy. The SC is still a driver's car. One you buy to look at. One you buy to drive.

Numbers That Matter

This Full Combination Worldwide
Single figures — possibly unique

Context is everything. The 930 Turbo was the headline act of 1979 — louder, wider, more violent in its power delivery. Not a single Turbo Targa left the factory in either 1978 or 1979. You could have the headline, or you could have the sky. You could not have both. The SC Targa pursued a different kind of excellence — one that has grown more valuable as the years have passed. In Ice Green Metallic, with factory Bilstein sports suspension, in European specification — this combination exists in numbers that can likely be counted on two hands worldwide. Possibly one.

The Market

What It Costs Now — and What That Window Means

In the early 2000s, a clean 911 SC could be acquired for $15,000 to $20,000. These were simply unfashionable — air-cooled Porsches without the RS heritage of the earlier cars or the Carrera badge of the later ones. The market had not yet understood that the SC's position — refined enough to use daily, raw enough to communicate, unencumbered at only 1,210 kilograms — was a combination that later 911s would spend decades trying to recreate and never quite manage.

Concours-condition 911 SC examples now fetch upwards of $80,000 to $100,000, while driver-quality cars range from $30,000 to $50,000. A 1982 SC sold for $515,000 in November 2025. A European-specification Targa in a special-order color with factory sports suspension has every reason to command a premium — and the pool of comparable examples is essentially empty.

Final Word

A Car Worth Finding

The 1979 Porsche 911 SC Targa in European specification — wearing Ice Green Metallic, breathing through an unconstrained 930/03, riding on factory Bilstein sports suspension — exists in a category of classic car that is simultaneously overlooked and irreplaceable. Overlooked because it lacks the horsepower headline of the 930 Turbo, the chrome-bumper romance of the earlier long-hood cars, and the raw numbers of the 3.2 Carrera that followed. Irreplaceable because nothing else offers exactly this combination: the purity of the uncompromised European engine, the open-air theatre of the Targa body, the engagement of unassisted steering and a car that weighs only 1,210 kilograms, and a colour that behaves like weather — different in every light, unforgettable in any of them.

Porsche built this car intending it to be the last 911. That existential pressure — the sense that this was a final statement, not a tentative one — gives the SC a character that subsequent, more commercially comfortable 911s took years to recover. The SC does not ask permission. It simply is, completely, itself. And in Ice Green Metallic, on a grey European morning, with the Targa top stowed and the 930/03 singing through its clean, uncatalyzed exhaust, it is one of the finest arguments for the internal combustion engine that the twentieth century produced.

At the end of a drive in this car you are fatigued. Not in the depleted, defeated way of a long motorway crossing in something modern and isolating. In the way you are fatigued after a proper physical effort that required your attention and rewarded your skill — like a warm workout, the pleasant ache of having used yourself well. You are energized to go again immediately. You are aware, as you switch off the flat-six and sit in the sudden quiet, that you accomplished something on the way here. That you got somewhere with grace and with genuine fun. That the journey mattered as much as the destination, in the way that only certain journeys and certain cars are capable of making true.

I have never finished a drive in this car and felt sad. Not once. That is not a small claim. As cars become more advanced, more managed, more insulated from the experience of driving — as they turn into very impressive, very safe, very comfortable appliances that happen to have wheels — I find myself turning to this car more, not less. It is a vacation from your other car. A vacation from the modern world and its perfectly optimized everything. From the screens and the algorithms and the helpful systems that catch your mistakes before you have had a chance to learn from them. This car makes no such offer. It offers only the road, the weight in your hands, the engine speaking behind you, and the sky above if you have had the good sense to remove the roof.

It really is a remarkable machine. The green does help.
